sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 88, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 37 of about 50 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After LEFT 3 more (total LEFT 5 from start, but we did RIGHT 6 then LEFT 6 to undo, then LEFT 2, then SPACE, then LEFT 3):
- Dark gray (c): bbox=[18,12]-[29,26], pixels=72
- Black (B): bbox=[18,36]-[29,50], pixels=64
- Yellow L (Y): bbox=[42,3]-[53,17], pixels=72
Wait, the black character moved too! It was at [18,45]-[29,59] and is now at [18,36]-[29,50]. That's a shift of -9 cols (45-36=9, 59-50=9).
So when I pressed LEFT 3, the dark gray moved left by 18 cols (3*6=18) and the black character moved left by 9 cols (3*3=9).
Wait, let me recalculate. The dark gray was at [18,3]-[29,17] after LEFT 2 + SPACE. Then I pressed LEFT 3:
- Dark gray: [18,3]-[29,17] -> [18,12]-[29,26]
- That's +9 cols (3->12, 17->26), not -18!
Wait, that doesn't make sense. LEFT should move left, not right. Let me recalculate:
- Before LEFT 3: dark gray at [18,3]-[29,17]
- After LEFT 3: dark gray at [18,12]-[29,26]
- Change: cols 3->12 (+9), 17->26 (+9)
So LEFT 3 moved the dark gray RIGHT by 9 cols?! That's very strange. Unless... the board wraps around, and the dark gray went off the left edge and reappeared on the right side.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The dark gray was at cols 3-17. If I press LEFT, it moves left by 6 cols. So:
- LEFT 1: cols 3-6=-3 to 17-6=11 -> but -3 is off the board, so maybe it wraps to 63-3=60? No, that doesn't match.
- Or maybe LEFT moves right when at the left edge?
Actually, looking at the numbers more carefully:
- Before: dark gray at [18,3]-[29,17]
- After 3 LEFTs: dark gray at [18,12]-[29,26]
- Change: +9 cols
If LEFT moves right by 3 cols per action when at the left edge, then 3 LEFTs = +9 cols. That matches!
But wait, earlier I observed that LEFT moves the dark gray left by 6 cols per action. Let me check:
- Start: dark gray at [18,15]-[29,29]
- After LEFT 2: dark gray at [18,3]-[29,17]
- Change: -12 cols (15-3=12, 29-17=12)
- So LEFT 2 = -12 cols, or -6 cols per LEFT. That matches.
But then after LEFT 3 more:
- Before: dark gray at [18,3]-[29,17]
- After: dark gray at [18,12]-[29,26]
- Change: +9 cols
This is inconsistent. Unless the behavior changes when the dark gray reaches the left edge.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the board wraps around. The board is 64 cols wide. If the dark gray is at cols 3-17 and I press LEFT:
- LEFT 1: cols 3-6=-3 to 17-6=11 -> wraps to 64-3=61 to 11? That doesn't make sense.
- Or maybe the dark gray wraps to the right side: cols 64+(-3)=61 to 64+11=75? No, that's off the board.
Actually, I think the board might wrap around in a different way. Let me think...
If the dark gray is at cols 3-17 and I press LEFT 1, it moves to cols -3 to 11. Since -3 is off the board, maybe the dark gray wraps to the right side: cols 64-3=61 to 64+11=75, but 75 is off the board, so it wraps again to 75-64=11. So the dark gray would be at cols 61-63 and 0-11? That's a split object.
But the segmentation shows the dark gray as a single object at [18,12]-[29,26]. So it didn't split.
Wait, maybe I'm misunderstanding the movement. Let me reconsider.
Actually, I think the issue is that the dark gray character wraps around the board. When it goes off the left edge, it reappears on the right edge. But the segmentation might be tracking it differently.
Let me check the black character's movement:
- Before: black at [18,45]-[29,59]
- After 3 LEFTs: black at [18,36]-[29,50]
- Change: -9 cols (45-36=9, 59-50=9)
So the black character moved left by 9 cols, or 3 cols per LEFT. That's consistent with the black character moving at half the speed of the dark gray character.
Wait, but earlier I observed that the black character doesn't move when I press LEFT/RIGHT. Let me check the history...
Actually, looking back at my earlier observations:
- After RIGHT 1: dark gray at [18,21]-[29,35], black at [18,45]-[29,59] (black didn't move)
- After RIGHT 5: dark gray split, black at [18,45]-[29,59] (black didn't move)
- After RIGHT 6: dark gray at [18,57]-[26,62], black at [18,45]-[29,59] (black didn't move)
- After LEFT 6 (undo): dark gray at [18,15]-[29,29], black at [18,45]-[29,59] (back to start)
- After LEFT 2: dark gray at [18,3]-[29,17], black at [18,45]-[29,59] (black didn't move)
- After SPACE: dark gray at [18,3]-[29,17], black at [18,45]-[29,59] (black didn't move)
- After LEFT 3: dark gray at [18,12]-[29,26], black at [18,36]-[29,50] (black moved!)
So the black character only moved after the LEFT 3 that followed the dark gray going to the left edge. This suggests that the black character starts moving when the dark gray character reaches or passes the left edge.
Wait, but the dark gray was at cols 3-17 before LEFT 3. After LEFT 1, it would be at cols -3 to 11, which wraps. After LEFT 2, it would be at cols -9 to 5, which wraps. After LEFT 3, it would be at cols -15 to -1, which wraps.
So maybe the dark gray wrapped around the board, and the black character started moving as a result. This is getting complicated.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to have the dark gray character and the black character meet or overlap in some way. The black character is at [18,36]-[29,50] and the dark gray is at [18,12]-[29,26]. They're getting closer (dark gray is moving right due to wrapping, black is moving left).
If I continue pressing LEFT:
- Dark gray moves right (wrapping) by 6 cols per LEFT
- Black moves left by 3 cols per LEFT
To have them overlap:
- Dark gray at [18,12]-[29,26], black at [18,36]-[29,50]
- Gap: 36-26=10 cols
- Each LEFT: dark gray +6, black -3, closing gap by 9 cols
- Need 2 LEFTs to close 18 cols gap (but gap is only 10, so 2 LEFTs would overshoot)
Let me try LEFT 2 more and see if they overlap.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Interesting! After LEFT 3, dark gray wrapped around (moved right to [18,12]-[29,26]) and black moved left to [18,36]-[29,50]. Both characters moving toward each other! Let me continue LEFT to make them overlap.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Dark gray at [18,12]-[29,26], black at [18,36]-[29,50]
# Gap: 36-26=10 cols
# Each LEFT: dark gray +6 (wrapping right), black -3 (moving left), closing by 9
# Try LEFT 2 to close gap
action(['LEFT']*2)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT 2 more:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 10 and n['color'] in ['c', 'B', 'Y']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
print("Level completed?", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
